quiz.title: Geometry: The Law of Sines and Law of Cosines
quiz.subtitle: Unit 8 Quiz 10: Extending Trigonometry to Non-Right Triangles (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Geometry: 8.10 The Law of Sines and Cosines (Introduction)

questions.question: In a non-right triangle, you are given two angles and one side (AAS or ASA). Which law should you use to find the remaining sides?
questions.explanation: The Law of Sines: sin(A)/a = sin(B)/b = sin(C)/c is used when you know two angles and any side, or two sides and a non-included angle. With two angles known, you can find the third angle (sum = 180°), then use the Law of Sines to find unknown sides.

questions.question: In △ABC, ∠A = 40°, ∠B = 60°, and side a (opposite ∠A) = 12. Find side b (opposite ∠B), rounded to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: Use Law of Sines: sin(40°)/12 = sin(60°)/b. Cross-multiplying: b = 12·sin(60°)/sin(40°). With a calculator: sin(60°) ≈ 0.8660, sin(40°) ≈ 0.6428. So b ≈ 12(0.8660)/(0.6428) ≈ 16.2. First find ∠C = 180° - 40° - 60° = 80°.

questions.question: In △PQR, ∠P = 35°, ∠Q = 75°, and side r (opposite ∠R) = 20. Find side p (opposite ∠P), rounded to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: First find ∠R = 180° - 35° - 75° = 70°. Use Law of Sines: sin(35°)/p = sin(70°)/20. Cross-multiplying: p = 20·sin(35°)/sin(70°). With a calculator: sin(35°) ≈ 0.5736, sin(70°) ≈ 0.9397. So p ≈ 20(0.5736)/(0.9397) ≈ 12.2.

questions.question: In △XYZ, ∠X = 50°, side x (opposite ∠X) = 15, and side y = 12. Find ∠Y, rounded to the nearest tenth of a degree. (Assume the triangle is acute.)
questions.explanation: Use Law of Sines: sin(50°)/15 = sin(Y)/12. Cross-multiplying: sin(Y) = 12·sin(50°)/15. With a calculator: sin(50°) ≈ 0.7660, so sin(Y) ≈ 12(0.7660)/15 ≈ 0.6128. Then Y = sin⁻¹(0.6128) ≈ 37.8°. Since y < x, Y < X, so this acute solution is valid.

questions.question: In a non-right triangle, you are given all three sides (SSS) and need to find an angle. Which law should you use?
questions.explanation: The Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C) is used when given three sides (SSS) to find any angle, or when given two sides and the included angle (SAS) to find the third side. Rearrange to isolate cos(C) when solving for an angle.

questions.question: In △ABC, side a = 8, side b = 10, and side c = 12. Find the measure of ∠C (the largest angle), rounded to the nearest tenth of a degree.
questions.explanation: Use Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C). Substituting: 144 = 64 + 100 - 2(8)(10)·cos(C) → 144 = 164 - 160·cos(C) → -20 = -160·cos(C) → cos(C) = 0.125. Then C = cos⁻¹(0.125) ≈ 82.8°.

questions.question: In △DEF, side d = 7, side e = 9, and side f = 11. Find the measure of ∠D (the smallest angle), rounded to the nearest tenth of a degree.
questions.explanation: Use Law of Cosines with side d: d² = e² + f² - 2ef·cos(D). Substituting: 49 = 81 + 121 - 2(9)(11)·cos(D) → 49 = 202 - 198·cos(D) → -153 = -198·cos(D) → cos(D) ≈ 0.7727. Then D = cos⁻¹(0.7727) ≈ 39.4°.

questions.question: In △JKL, ∠J = 55°, side k = 14, and side l = 18. Find side j (opposite ∠J), rounded to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: Use Law of Cosines (SAS): j² = k² + l² - 2kl·cos(J). Substituting: j² = 196 + 324 - 2(14)(18)·cos(55°). With a calculator: cos(55°) ≈ 0.5736. So j² = 520 - 504(0.5736) = 520 - 289.1 = 230.9. Then j = √230.9 ≈ 15.2.

questions.question: In △MNO, ∠M = 42°, side n = 25, and side o = 30. Find side m (opposite ∠M), rounded to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: Use Law of Cosines (SAS): m² = n² + o² - 2no·cos(M). Substituting: m² = 625 + 900 - 2(25)(30)·cos(42°). With a calculator: cos(42°) ≈ 0.7431. So m² = 1525 - 1500(0.7431) = 1525 - 1114.7 = 410.3. Then m = √410.3 ≈ 20.3.

questions.question: A triangular park has sides measuring 150 feet, 200 feet, and 250 feet. What is the measure of the largest angle, rounded to the nearest degree?
questions.explanation: The longest side is 250 ft. Use Law of Cosines: 250² = 150² + 200² - 2(150)(200)·cos(C). Substituting: 62,500 = 22,500 + 40,000 - 60,000·cos(C) → 62,500 = 62,500 - 60,000·cos(C) → 0 = -60,000·cos(C) → cos(C) = 0 → C = 90°. This is a scaled 3-4-5 right triangle!

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines. You know when to use each law and can solve for unknown sides and angles in non-right triangles.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: use Law of Sines when given two angles and a side (AAS/ASA) or two sides and a non-included angle (SSA). Use Law of Cosines when given three sides (SSS) or two sides and the included angle (SAS).
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) Law of Sines: sin(A)/a = sin(B)/b = sin(C)/c, (2) Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C), (3) choosing the correct law based on what is given, (4) rearranging correctly to solve for the unknown.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! For non-right triangles, SOH CAH TOA and the Pythagorean Theorem don't work. Use the Law of Sines when you know angles, and the Law of Cosines when you know sides. The Law of Cosines generalizes the Pythagorean Theorem — notice it reduces to a² + b² = c² when the angle is 90°.
